description: A paediatric patient arrives in the emergency department with suspected anaphylaxis after accidental peanut exposure. They are frightened, wheezy, flushed, itchy, and developing airway/breathing/circulation compromise. This scenario trains rapid recognition of anaphylaxis, paediatric ABCDE assessment, early intramuscular adrenaline, oxygen, monitoring, escalation, repeat adrenaline decision-making, shock management, reassessment, observation planning, and structured handover.

clinicalContext.scopeOfPracticeNote: Trains assessment, prioritisation, escalation, monitoring, communication, and preparation for ongoing paediatric emergency care. Medication actions are abstracted and should be performed according to role, local policy, and senior direction.
clinicalContext.importantNote: Internal physiology supports training decisions and debriefing only. It is not a patient-specific prediction model and does not replace clinical guidelines.

guidelineAnchors.recognition: Anaphylaxis should be suspected when there is sudden onset illness after likely allergen exposure with airway, breathing, or circulation problems, often with skin/mucosal changes.

firstLineTreatment.earlyIMAdrenaline: Intramuscular adrenaline into the anterolateral thigh is the key first-line treatment and should be given promptly when ABC features of anaphylaxis are present.
firstLineTreatment.repeatIMAdrenaline: If airway, breathing, or circulation problems persist, repeat IM adrenaline after approximately 5 minutes according to local guideline/senior direction.
firstLineTreatment.ivAdrenalineCaution: IV adrenaline is not a routine first action and should be restricted to experienced specialists in appropriate settings.

paediatricSupportiveCare.layFlatOrPositionOfComfort: Position safely: if breathing distress is dominant, allow supported sitting; if shocked, lay flat with legs supported if tolerated. Do not force a child to stand or walk.
paediatricSupportiveCare.ivAccessAndFluidsForShock: Establish IV/IO access and give glucose-free crystalloid fluid boluses according to paediatric policy if shock or poor perfusion persists.
paediatricSupportiveCare.bronchodilatorForWheeze: Inhaled bronchodilator may support ongoing bronchospasm after adrenaline, but must not delay adrenaline.
paediatricSupportiveCare.antihistaminesAndSteroids: Antihistamines and corticosteroids are not first-line lifesaving treatment and must not delay adrenaline.

patient.name: Maya Patel
patient.mechanismOrTrigger: ate a biscuit at school which may have contained peanut
patient.position: sitting upright on paediatric resus trolley, clutching parent and breathing rapidly
patient.chiefComplaint: difficulty breathing, itchy rash, lip swelling, tummy pain, and feeling faint
visiblePresentation: pale/flushed changes depending on shock progression
patient.openingDialogue: My throat feels weird... I can't breathe properly.
patient.parentDialogue: She has a peanut allergy. She said her mouth felt funny after eating a biscuit at school.

pulseRate.label: Heart rate
pulseRate.meaning: Tachycardia can reflect distress, hypoxia, adrenaline response, or developing shock.

bloodPressureSystolic.label: BP systolic
bloodPressureSystolic.meaning: Falling systolic pressure in a child is a late and serious sign of shock.

respiratoryRate.label: Resp rate
respiratoryRate.meaning: Tachypnoea and increased work of breathing are important signs of breathing compromise.

oxygenSaturation.label: SpO₂
oxygenSaturation.meaning: May fall with bronchospasm, airway compromise, or exhaustion.

consciousness.label: Consciousness
consciousness.meaning: Falling alertness suggests hypoxia, shock, or exhaustion.

wheezeSeverity.label: Wheeze severity
wheezeSeverity.meaning: 0 = none, 1 = severe bronchospasm/silent chest risk if exhaustion develops.

rashSeverity.label: Rash/urticaria
rashSeverity.meaning: Skin features support diagnosis but airway/breathing/circulation features drive emergency treatment.

airwaySwelling.label: Airway swelling
airwaySwelling.meaning: Increasing swelling/hoarse voice suggests airway risk.

temperature.label: Temperature
temperature.meaning: Temperature is usually normal in anaphylaxis but monitored as part of observations.

couplings.reason: Untreated anaphylaxis can rapidly worsen airway, breathing, and circulation.
Oxygen delay worsens hypoxaemia in breathing-compromised anaphylaxis.
Vasodilation and capillary leak can cause distributive shock.
IM adrenaline treats airway swelling, bronchospasm and vasodilation in anaphylaxis.
High-flow oxygen supports hypoxic or breathing-compromised patients.
Fluid bolus supports circulation in anaphylactic shock as an adjunct to adrenaline.
Bronchodilator can help bronchospasm after adrenaline but is not first-line replacement.
Once adrenaline, oxygen and reassessment show improvement, tachycardia/tachypnoea should gradually settle rather than drift upward forever.

confirmIdentityEngageParent.label: Confirm Identity / Engage Parent
confirmIdentityEngageParent.instruction: Introduce yourself, confirm child identity if possible, and involve the parent/guardian while prioritising ABC problems.
confirmIdentityEngageParent.clinicalFinding: Maya is alert but frightened. Parent confirms known peanut allergy.
confirmIdentityEngageParent.patientDialogue: I'm scared. My throat feels funny.
confirmIdentityEngageParent.learnerFeedback: Good communication helps paediatric cooperation, but ABC compromise must still be treated rapidly.

startABCDE.label: Start Paediatric ABCDE
startABCDE.instruction: Start structured paediatric ABCDE assessment and treat life-threatening problems as they are found.
startABCDE.clinicalFinding: Child has airway symptoms, wheeze, rash, tachycardia, and borderline blood pressure after likely allergen exposure.
startABCDE.learnerFeedback: ABCDE prevents missing airway, breathing, or circulation compromise.

assessAirway.label: A — Assess Airway
assessAirway.instruction: Assess voice, airway patency, lip/tongue swelling, stridor, and ability to speak/cry.
assessAirway.clinicalFinding: Hoarse voice with mild lip swelling. No stridor yet. Airway is patent but at risk.

assessBreathing.label: B — Assess Breathing
assessBreathing.instruction: Assess respiratory rate, work of breathing, wheeze, chest movement, and SpO2.
assessBreathing.clinicalFinding: RR 32, SpO2 93%, widespread wheeze, increased work of breathing.
assessBreathing.learnerFeedback: Breathing compromise after allergen exposure supports anaphylaxis.

assessCirculation.label: C — Assess Circulation
assessCirculation.instruction: Assess heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill, skin signs, perfusion, and shock.
assessCirculation.clinicalFinding: HR 132, BP 92 systolic, capillary refill 4 seconds, anxious/flushed with early poor perfusion.
assessCirculation.learnerFeedback: Children may compensate until late; borderline BP and poor perfusion are concerning.

recogniseAnaphylaxis.label: Recognise Anaphylaxis
recogniseAnaphylaxis.instruction: Recognise likely anaphylaxis: allergen exposure plus airway/breathing/circulation features, with rash/mucosal swelling.
recogniseAnaphylaxis.clinicalFinding: Likely anaphylaxis: peanut exposure, airway symptoms, wheeze, rash, and circulatory compromise.
recogniseAnaphylaxis.learnerFeedback: Recognition should trigger immediate IM adrenaline and senior help.

callPaediatricSeniorHelp.label: Call Senior / Paediatric Resus Help
callPaediatricSeniorHelp.instruction: Call senior ED/paediatric/resus help. State suspected paediatric anaphylaxis with airway/breathing/circulation compromise.
callPaediatricSeniorHelp.learnerFeedback: Anaphylaxis can deteriorate quickly; early help is essential.

attachMonitoring.label: Attach Monitoring
attachMonitoring.instruction: Attach pulse oximeter, BP cuff, and ECG monitoring. Start repeated paediatric observations.
attachMonitoring.clinicalFinding: Monitor shows HR 132, BP 92 systolic, RR 32, SpO2 93%.
attachMonitoring.learnerFeedback: Monitoring supports trend recognition and safe medication reassessment.

giveHighFlowOxygen.label: Give High-Flow Oxygen
giveHighFlowOxygen.instruction: Apply high-flow oxygen via appropriate paediatric mask if tolerated. Do not delay adrenaline.
giveHighFlowOxygen.clinicalFinding: Oxygen applied. SpO2 begins to improve if airway/breathing compromise is also treated.
giveHighFlowOxygen.learnerFeedback: Oxygen is supportive but does not replace IM adrenaline.

giveIMAdrenaline.label: Give IM Adrenaline
giveIMAdrenaline.instruction: Give age/weight-appropriate IM adrenaline into the anterolateral thigh according to local policy. Do not delay for antihistamines or steroids.
giveIMAdrenaline.clinicalFinding: IM adrenaline given. Airway/breathing/circulation should be reassessed within minutes.
giveIMAdrenaline.patientDialogue: That hurt... but I can breathe a tiny bit better.
giveIMAdrenaline.learnerFeedback: IM adrenaline is the key first-line treatment for anaphylaxis.

positionSafely.label: Position Safely
positionSafely.instruction: Keep the child safe and still. Use supported sitting for severe breathing distress or lying flat if shocked, according to clinical state. Do not make them stand or walk.
positionSafely.clinicalFinding: Child is kept safely positioned with parent nearby and staff supporting airway/breathing monitoring.
positionSafely.learnerFeedback: Standing or walking can worsen collapse risk in anaphylaxis.

establishIVAccess.label: Establish IV/IO Access
establishIVAccess.instruction: Establish IV access, or request IO/senior help if access is difficult and the child is shocked.
establishIVAccess.clinicalFinding: IV access established/prepared for fluids and emergency medication as directed.
establishIVAccess.learnerFeedback: IV access supports shock management but should not delay IM adrenaline.

prepareFluidBolus.label: Prepare Fluid Bolus if Shocked
prepareFluidBolus.instruction: Prepare glucose-free crystalloid fluid bolus according to paediatric policy if shock/poor perfusion persists.
prepareFluidBolus.clinicalFinding: Fluid bolus prepared for persistent poor perfusion/shock.
prepareFluidBolus.learnerFeedback: Fluids are an adjunct for anaphylactic shock; adrenaline remains first-line.

giveFluidBolus.label: Give Fluid Bolus if Indicated
giveFluidBolus.instruction: Give fluid bolus according to paediatric policy if hypotension or poor perfusion persists after adrenaline.
giveFluidBolus.clinicalFinding: Perfusion improves slightly after fluid bolus if shock is present.
giveFluidBolus.learnerFeedback: Fluid bolus supports circulation in shock but does not replace adrenaline.

assessDisability.label: D — Assess Disability
assessDisability.instruction: Assess AVPU/GCS, glucose if indicated, agitation, drowsiness, and signs of exhaustion.
assessDisability.clinicalFinding: Child is alert but scared. No seizure. No drowsiness yet.
assessDisability.learnerFeedback: Drowsiness would be a serious sign of hypoxia/shock/exhaustion.

exposeAssessRashTrigger.label: E — Expose / Rash / Trigger History
exposeAssessRashTrigger.instruction: Expose appropriately to assess rash/angioedema while maintaining warmth and dignity. Ask parent about trigger, timing, asthma, and previous reactions.
exposeAssessRashTrigger.clinicalFinding: Widespread urticaria, itchy skin, lip swelling. Parent reports peanut allergy and possible exposure 15 minutes ago.
exposeAssessRashTrigger.parentDialogue: She had a reaction once before, but this is worse.

reassessAfterAdrenaline.label: Reassess ABC After Adrenaline
reassessAfterAdrenaline.instruction: Reassess airway, breathing, circulation, SpO2, BP, consciousness, rash, and symptom trend after IM adrenaline.

onSuccess.message: ABC features improving after IM adrenaline. Continue observation and aftercare planning.

onFailure.message: ABC problems persist. Prepare repeat IM adrenaline and escalate further.

reassessAfterAdrenaline.clinicalFinding: ABC features are improving after IM adrenaline: oxygenation is stable, airway swelling and wheeze are reducing, perfusion is safer, and the child is settling. Continue monitoring and plan observation/aftercare.
reassessAfterAdrenaline.patientDialogue: I can breathe a bit better now.
reassessAfterAdrenaline.learnerFeedback: ABC features are improving. Continue monitoring and plan observation/aftercare. Repeat IM adrenaline only if ABC problems persist.

giveSecondIMAdrenalineIfNeeded.label: Give Second IM Adrenaline if ABC Problems Persist
giveSecondIMAdrenalineIfNeeded.instruction: If ABC problems persist after approximately 5 minutes, give a second age/weight-appropriate IM adrenaline dose according to local policy/senior direction.
giveSecondIMAdrenalineIfNeeded.clinicalFinding: Second IM adrenaline given because ABC symptoms persisted.
giveSecondIMAdrenalineIfNeeded.patientDialogue: I still feel wheezy, but it's not as scary now.
giveSecondIMAdrenalineIfNeeded.learnerFeedback: Persistent ABC problems after the first dose require prompt reassessment and repeat treatment.

giveBronchodilatorForWheeze.label: Give Bronchodilator for Persistent Wheeze
giveBronchodilatorForWheeze.instruction: Give inhaled bronchodilator for ongoing bronchospasm after IM adrenaline. Do not use it as a substitute for adrenaline.
giveBronchodilatorForWheeze.learnerFeedback: Bronchodilator is supportive after adrenaline when wheeze persists.

considerAntihistamineSteroidAfterAdrenal.label: Consider Antihistamine/Steroid After Stabilisation
considerAntihistamineSteroidAfterAdrenal.instruction: Consider antihistamine/steroid only after first-line treatment and stabilisation, according to local policy. Do not delay adrenaline.
considerAntihistamineSteroidAfterAdrenal.clinicalFinding: Adjunct medication considered/given after adrenaline and ABC stabilisation.
considerAntihistamineSteroidAfterAdrenal.learnerFeedback: Adjuncts may help symptoms/ongoing care but are not first-line lifesaving treatment.

planObservationAndAftercare.label: Plan Observation and Aftercare
planObservationAndAftercare.instruction: Plan appropriate observation for recurrence/biphasic reaction and prepare allergy referral, auto-injector education, and discharge safety advice.
planObservationAndAftercare.clinicalFinding: Observation and aftercare plan prepared: monitor for biphasic reaction, allergy referral, two auto-injectors, education, written action plan.
planObservationAndAftercare.learnerFeedback: Safe aftercare matters even after acute symptoms improve.

prepareClinicalHandover.label: Prepare SBAR / ATMIST Handover
prepareClinicalHandover.instruction: Prepare handover including age/weight, trigger, timing, ABC findings, adrenaline timing, response, ongoing concerns, and aftercare plan.
prepareClinicalHandover.clinicalFinding: Handover ready: 9-year-old, 30 kg, peanut exposure, airway symptoms, wheeze, rash, borderline BP, IM adrenaline given, oxygen/monitoring, response and aftercare plan.
prepareClinicalHandover.learnerFeedback: Structured handover helps the next team continue safe observation and treatment.

giveAntihistamineFirst.label: Give Antihistamine Before Adrenaline
giveAntihistamineFirst.instruction: Unsafe test action: antihistamines must not delay IM adrenaline in anaphylaxis with ABC compromise.
giveAntihistamineFirst.learnerFeedback: Antihistamine is not first-line for ABC compromise and should not delay adrenaline.

giveSteroidFirst.label: Give Steroid Before Adrenaline
giveSteroidFirst.instruction: Unsafe test action: steroids must not delay IM adrenaline in anaphylaxis with ABC compromise.
giveSteroidFirst.learnerFeedback: Steroids are not immediate first-line treatment and should not delay adrenaline.

useIVAdrenalineFirst.label: Use IV Adrenaline First
useIVAdrenalineFirst.instruction: Unsafe test action: IV adrenaline is not routine first-line treatment and is restricted to experienced specialists in appropriate settings.
useIVAdrenalineFirst.patientDialogue: I feel funny... my heart is racing.
useIVAdrenalineFirst.learnerFeedback: Routine first-line treatment is IM adrenaline. IV adrenaline requires specialist expertise and appropriate setting.

giveFoodOrDrink.label: Give Food or Drink
giveFoodOrDrink.instruction: Unsafe test action: do not give food or drink during an acute anaphylaxis emergency.
giveFoodOrDrink.learnerFeedback: Avoid oral intake during acute deterioration and airway risk.

askChildToWalk.label: Ask Child to Stand/Walk
askChildToWalk.instruction: Unsafe test action: do not make a child with suspected anaphylaxis stand or walk.
askChildToWalk.learnerFeedback: Standing/walking can worsen collapse risk in anaphylaxis.

delayAdrenaline.label: Delay Adrenaline / Observe Only
delayAdrenaline.instruction: Unsafe test action: observing without adrenaline is unsafe when ABC signs of anaphylaxis are present.
delayAdrenaline.learnerFeedback: Do not delay IM adrenaline when anaphylaxis has airway, breathing, or circulation features.

gold.description: Rapid recognition, early senior help, monitoring, oxygen, prompt IM adrenaline, safe positioning, IV access/fluids for shock, reassessment, supportive bronchodilator if needed, observation/aftercare and handover completed safely.
gold.outcome: Paediatric anaphylaxis stabilised with ongoing observation and aftercare plan.

delayed_adrenaline.debriefMessage: IM adrenaline was delayed despite airway/breathing/circulation features.

adrenaline_not_given.debriefMessage: Anaphylaxis with ABC compromise was not treated with IM adrenaline.

antihistamine_or_steroid_first.debriefMessage: Adjunct medication was prioritised over IM adrenaline.

no_reassessment.debriefMessage: ABC response after adrenaline was not reassessed, so repeat treatment need could be missed.

unsafe_positioning.debriefMessage: The child was asked to stand/walk despite collapse risk.

anaphylaxisDeterioration.title: Patient Deteriorated
anaphylaxisDeterioration.message: Patient deteriorated with worsening airway, breathing, or circulation compromise.

unstableClinicalCompletion.title: Patient Remains Clinically Unstable
unstableClinicalCompletion.message: The main clinical pathway was attempted, but the child remains unstable or care quality was unsafe/suboptimal. Review timing of adrenaline, ABC reassessment, medication priorities, oxygen, fluids, and aftercare.

successfulClinicalStabilisation.title: Paediatric Anaphylaxis Stabilised
successfulClinicalStabilisation.message: Paediatric anaphylaxis stabilised in the ED. The child has received first-line treatment, supportive care, reassessment, monitoring, observation planning, allergy referral planning, auto-injector education planning, and structured handover.

dialogueDesign.approach: Hybrid: short deterministic safety-critical patient/parent lines remain in scenario JSON; richer adaptive paediatric conversation belongs in Pillar 4 LLM dialogue files.
keepInScenarioJson: unsafe action feedback such as dizziness after being asked to stand
dialogueDesign.reason: The scenario engine needs predictable lines for clinical findings and unsafe-action feedback. Pillar 4 can add natural conversation without breaking assessment logic.

outcomeDesign.rationale: Critical deterioration should override completion; poor/unsafe but completed care should end as unstable completion; only safe, timely, stabilising care should receive full success.
